nativeRange.habitat: The Congo Puffer is found in slow-moving to sluggish river channels and marginal areas of the Congo Basin, over sandy and silty substrates covered with accumulated leaf litter and woody debris. The water in these habitats is typically turbid, soft, and acidic.

decorPreferences: deep fine sand substrate (8 cm minimum — mandatory for burial behaviour)
minimal or no gravel (sharp edges injure burrowing fish)
driftwood and bogwood for visual cover
leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, oak leaves) to mimic natural biotope
low to moderate flow — species inhabits sluggish water
subdued lighting; floating plants to diffuse overhead light

diet.notes: The Congo Puffer is a strict live-prey ambush predator, and weaning individuals onto prepared or frozen foods is rarely successful and should not be expected. The core diet consists of live invertebrates: pond snails, ramshorn snails, and mystery snails are particularly important because their hard shells help wear down the beak teeth, which grow continuously throughout the fish's life. Dental overgrowth is a serious health risk in specimens fed exclusively soft foods — regular access to hard-shelled snails is essential. Live ghost shrimp, river shrimp, and earthworms round out a varied diet. Whole feeder fish such as guppies or minnows are accepted, but they are nutritionally incomplete as a sole food source and should be used only for occasional variety. Gut-loaded, vitamin-rich invertebrates are preferable to feeder fish. Frozen bloodworm and mussel are occasionally accepted by settled specimens, but cannot be relied upon as a dietary staple. Feeding every two to three days is appropriate; overfeeding leads to obesity.

avoidWith: all other fish (will ambush and consume or mutilate any tankmate regardless of size)
invertebrates (snails and shrimp used as live food only, not long-term tankmates)
conspecifics (extreme intraspecific aggression; multiple individuals cannot be safely housed together)
bottom-dwelling species sharing the substrate layer

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Tetrani; 800x298. Sharp full-body lateral view of a live specimen resting on fine sand, clearly showing the chunky body, upward-facing mouth, green eye and mottled tan/brown camouflage colouration. Best overall representation.

Photo by Zaire; 1985x598. Specimen buried in sand with only the eye and snout exposed, perfectly illustrating the ambush-burial behaviour described in the care notes. Sharp; kept as a behavioural extra.
Photo by Zaire; 1507x1441. Sharp frontal face close-up showing the upward-set eyes and superior mouth; body softly out of focus behind, so kept as a non-recommended feature detail.

summary: The Congo Puffer is a specialist-only ambush predator from the Congo Basin that buries its entire body in fine sand with only its eyes exposed, lunging upward to engulf prey. Its colour-changing camouflage, aggressive temperament, and strict live-food requirements make it suitable only for an experienced single-species setup.
careNotes:
The Congo Puffer (Tetraodon miurus) is one of the most specialised freshwater pufferfish in the hobby and demands a single-species setup with no exceptions. In the wild it inhabits sluggish, turbid reaches of the Congo River system, burying itself completely in fine sandy or silty substrate with only the upward-facing eyes and the tip of the snout exposed. It strikes explosively upward when prey passes overhead — this ambush lunge is fast enough to take fish substantially larger than itself. No tankmate is safe regardless of size, speed, or armour.

Substrate is non-negotiable: a minimum of 8 cm of very fine-grained sand (grain size under 1 mm) must be provided across the full tank footprint. Coarser substrate or gravel prevents burial and causes chronic stress and physical injury to the abdomen and fins. A tank of at least 110 L with a 90 cm footprint is a working minimum for a single adult; larger is better.

Water parameters should reflect the soft, slightly acidic conditions of the Congo Basin: pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–15, kH 1–8, temperature 24–28°C. Excellent filtration and regular water changes are essential — puffers are heavy waste producers and are sensitive to elevated ammonia and nitrite. Low to moderate flow replicates the sluggish natural habitat.

Feeding exclusively on live invertebrates is normal for this species; owners must maintain a colony of live feeder snails as a staple. Hard-shelled snails (ramshorn, pond, mystery) are essential to prevent beak overgrowth — the teeth grow continuously and will require trimming by a vet if hard prey is not provided regularly. Feeding every 2–3 days is appropriate; daily overfeeding causes obesity and fatty liver disease. Whole feeder fish should be limited due to thiaminase risk and incomplete nutrition.

The Congo Puffer is a master of colour change, shifting rapidly between mottled brown, grey, rust, and cream to match the substrate — this camouflage is most visible in tanks with varied natural-toned sand. Specimens that feel exposed may display pallor or erratic movement, indicating stress. Puffing up (inflating) is a severe stress response and should not be deliberately triggered.

Like all Tetraodontidae this species contains tetrodotoxin concentrated in the skin and organs. It is not a risk under normal aquarium conditions but should never be handled without gloves, and deceased specimens must be disposed of safely away from children and pets.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been reliably documented for Tetraodon miurus. No captive spawning reports exist in the mainstream aquarium literature. The extreme aggression between individuals makes even temporary pairing attempts dangerous. This species is considered not bred in captivity for practical hobby purposes.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Tetraodon miurus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 15 cm), temperature range (24–28°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness, native range (Congo Basin), habitat description, diet (live invertebrates; will not accept prepared foods reliably), minimum tank size, substrate requirements (fine sand, deep bed), aggression notes, and solitary-only keeping requirement.

additional.name: FishBase — Tetraodon miurus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Tetraodontidae, Tetraodontiformes), adult size (to 15 cm TL), native range (Congo River system, DRC and Republic of Congo), water temperature, and diet data. Confirmed order Tetraodontiformes.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pea Puffer and Puffer Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: Used for general pufferfish husbandry context: diet (snails, shrimp, live prey), beak overgrowth concerns, live-food dependency, and solitary housing requirements. Congo Puffer not covered as a dedicated species; cross-referenced general puffer guidance with species-specific SeriouslyFish data.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Congo Puffer (Tetraodon miurus)
additional.notes: Supplementary source for ambush predator behaviour, sand-burial depth requirements, colour-change camouflage description, and single-species tank advisory. Confirmed inability to keep with any tankmates and live-food-only diet in practice.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Hobbyist Magazine — Pufferfish of the Congo
additional.notes: Used for Congo Basin biotope context, description of substrate-burying ambush behaviour, and specialist-level care requirements. Corroborated 8 cm+ sand depth requirement and live-invertebrate diet as non-negotiable.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive spawning records exist. Extreme intraspecific aggression prevents pairing attempts under normal aquarium conditions.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Strict species-only tank required (no viable tankmates). Ambush predator that buries in fine sand and lunges at any moving target. Mandatory 8cm+ fine sand substrate; specialist single-fish setup.
